### Post by z0phi3l on 2007-11-09
I have the latest nvidia drivers installed instead of using the restricted driver, I needed the newer ones to stop from crashing while playing WoW. 

WoW is fine but I can't enable the extra effects, every time I try it insists on using the restricted drivers.


My xorg.conf looks fine  

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig

    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY

    Option         "Type" "cursor"
    Option         "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 70.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 160.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Generic Video Card"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Generic Video Card"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "AddARGBVisuals" "True"
    Option         "NoLogo" "True"
    Option         "AddARGBGLXVisuals" "True"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "nvidia-auto-select"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
    Option         "Composite" "Enable"
EndSection
```

Any suggestions?

### Post by Zorael on 2007-11-09
Does it output anything of interest if you try to launch Compiz from a terminal?

```
$ compiz --loose-binding &
```

### Post by Zorael on 2007-11-10
I'm *not at all* sure this will help with it demanding the restricted driver. I'm not even sure I completely understand what you mean, since the nvidia-glx(-new) package supplies the nvidia driver, much like installing it from the official package and/or Envy does. It shouldn't be able to discern any difference, no?


Regardless, this might help with your terminal output errors. Sort of off topic - don't let this make you stray from the topic, following posters.

Compiz has some known issues with reading settings from KDE/Gnome (gconf) Backends.

Try opening up the settings manager (ccsm), go to Preferences, Export your profile, switch to Flat-file Configuration Backend, and then Import it again.

Then try starting Compiz with:
```
$ compiz --loose-binding ccp &
```
The **ccp** argument will make it use its own configuration file format.

If it still doesn't work, you could try deleting your profile and starting anew. Maybe something went wrong somewhere.

Also, you may want to add:
```
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "true"
```
...to the Screen section of your xorg.conf. And/or the Device section. I never got a clear answer on that. I just put it in both. :>


My script:
```
zorael@azrael:~$ cat /usr/bin/compiz.start
#!/bin/bash

# invoke compiz with KDE and Nvidia tweaks. pass arguments to compiz and leave terminal control to the user
__GL_YIELD="NOTHING" compiz --loose-binding --ignore-desktop-hints ccp $@ &
```

---
